symbols.S(x): x is in servitude to others
symbols.G(x): x is an agent claiming godlike authority
symbols.CFS(X): X is a consistent formal axiomatic system
symbols.AI_E: AI has an ethical alignment system
symbols.Defect(x,y): x defects (violates property of) y in last round

formalizations.natural: Liberty is identical to individual property rights.
formalizations.notes: Definitional biconditional; the theory's central definition.

formalizations.natural: Denial of free will is self-refuting.
formalizations.notes: Performative contradiction: asserting ¬FW requires FW to make the assertion.

formalizations.natural: Eliminating Tawhid proliferates axioms and leads to totalitarianism.
formalizations.notes: Chain: no Tawhid → infinite axiom proliferation → no consistent system → mysticism → no liberty.

formalizations.natural: Liberty → Property Rights → Religion (master chain).
formalizations.notes: The arrow PR → R is conditional on stability requirement.

formalizations.natural: The formal system of liberty is equivalent to religion.
formalizations.notes: Biconditional: any CFS for liberty is religion; religion (authentic) is a CFS for liberty.

formalizations.natural: Liberty is unstable without Resurrection.
formalizations.notes: Game-theoretic: without unknown terminal, backward induction produces defection.

formalizations.natural: A valid formal system must be internally consistent.
formalizations.notes: Standard consistency requirement. Capital-C Consistent in Gödel sense: no proof of ⊥.

formalizations.natural: Human free will exists and is universally presupposed.
formalizations.notes: Universal quantification over humans. Second conjunct: performative presupposition.

formalizations.natural: Tawhid: no human has the right to deprive another of free will.
formalizations.notes: Tawhid operationalized as universal prohibition on depriving others of free will.

formalizations.natural: Tawhid guarantees liberty by eliminating servitude to other-than-God.
formalizations.notes: T eliminates servitude; absence of servitude entails liberty.

formalizations.natural: Rule of Taslīṭ: persons are sovereign over their property.
formalizations.notes: Exclusive sovereignty; no third party has rightful control over another's property.

formalizations.natural: Religion is the antithesis of the state.
formalizations.notes: Mutual exclusion: no system can be simultaneously state (monopoly-coercion) and liberty-CFS.

formalizations.natural: Democracy cannot legitimize violating individual rights.
formalizations.notes: Individual rights are outside the domain of democratic legitimation.

formalizations.natural: Free will is necessarily presupposed by all rational agents.
formalizations.notes: Necessity modal: in all possible worlds where rational agents exist, they presuppose free will.

formalizations.natural: There is no possible world in which free will is coherently denied.
formalizations.notes: Impossibility of coherent free will denial across all possible worlds.

formalizations.natural: The theory of liberty is the only possible consistent formal system for AI ethics.
formalizations.notes: FRAGILE: this is the uniqueness claim. The modal necessity is the weakest link. Requires proof that no alternative CFS exists.

formalizations.natural: Resurrection (unknown terminal) is possibly true and if true, stabilizes liberty.
formalizations.notes: Author does not claim Resurrection is necessarily true; only that it is agreement-admissible. Modal possibility captures this.

formalizations.natural: No compulsion in religion: it is forbidden to force religion on anyone.
formalizations.notes: Deontic prohibition: coercing religious acceptance is forbidden.

formalizations.natural: It is forbidden for anyone to violate another's property rights.
formalizations.notes: Universal deontic prohibition on property violation.

formalizations.natural: Market intervention is forbidden because it is equivalent to servitude.
formalizations.notes: Deontic transfer: if servitude is forbidden and intervention = servitude, intervention is forbidden.

formalizations.natural: Mysticism is forbidden as it separates persons from their rights.
formalizations.notes: Conditional deontic: membership in mystical systems is forbidden because of what such systems do.

formalizations.natural: A consistent formal system is necessary and sufficient for true religion.
formalizations.notes: Biconditional: authentic religion IS a consistent formal system for liberty.

formalizations.natural: Minimal axiom set: for any two systems solving the same problem, prefer fewer axioms.
formalizations.notes: Principle of parsimony applied to axiomatic systems. Grounds the Tawhid-over-alternatives argument.

formalizations.natural: If religion is a CFS, it can be encoded for AI alignment.
formalizations.notes: Existence claim: some encoding procedure maps CFS(R) to AI-alignment specification.

formalizations.natural: Objective truth exists independently of any observer.
formalizations.notes: Hidden axiom; required for 'agreement-admissible' to mean something non-trivial.

formalizations.natural: Gödel's theorems apply to the theory's axiomatic system.
formalizations.notes: FRAGILE: this existence claim is the weakest formalization. 'SufficientlyRich' requires Robinson arithmetic as a subsystem, which is unproven for social axiomatic systems.

coverage_assessment.propositional: Core chain formalized; sufficient for SAT-solver input with translation.
coverage_assessment.first_order: Universal quantifications over humans and systems captured; sufficient for FOL theorem provers.
coverage_assessment.modal: Necessity/possibility of liberty claims captured; requires S5 semantics.
coverage_assessment.deontic: Prohibition/permission structure of property rights captured; requires Standard Deontic Logic or Anderson's reduction.
known_gaps: The Resurrection game-theory argument needs temporal logic (LTL or CTL) to capture 'last-round'.
The Mahdism terminal condition needs a temporal operator for 'unknown future time T*'.
The Iran civilizational claims need a historical-process logic (interval logic or dynamic logic).
